title: A Portfolio Approach to Global Imbalances
abstract: We use a portfolio-based framework to understand what drives the decline of the U.S. net foreign asset (NFA) position and the reversal in returns earned on the U.S. NFA (exorbitant privilege). We show that global savings gluts and monetary policies widened the U.S. NFA position, while investor demand shifts partially offset this widening. Moreover, U.S. privilege declined after 2010, in line with increasing foreign demand for U.S. equity. We also highlight a quantity dimension of the U.S. privilege: The U.S. can issue substantially more debt than other countries for a given yield increase.

article.introduction:
In recent decades, the netexternal portfolio position of the U.S. has become substantially more negative. At the end of 2019, non-U.S. investors held 20.4 trillion dollars worth of U.S. external liabilities. By contrast, U.S. investors held only 8.7 trillion dollars in foreign investment assets.1This difference between external portfolio assets and liabilities resulted in a −$11.7 trillion net foreign asset (NFA) position for the U.S. in 2019, which amounted to 55% of U.S. GDP.2This is a substantial decrease from an NFA position of −$1.8 trillion in 2002, or 16% of U.S. GDP. The solid line in Figure1shows this downward trend. The sustained net capital flows into U.S. financial markets are a manifestation ofglobal imbalances(Lane and Milesi-Ferretti (2007), Gourinchas and Rey (2014)). These imbalances play an important role in understanding the international financial system, as they are closely tied to the global risk-sharing arrangement and the unique international position of the U.S.
  Changes in the U.S. external portfolio are driven by both the quantity of capital flows into and out of the U.S. and the returns on the existing portfolio. A prominent view holds that the U.S. earns a higher return on its external assets relative to what it pays to foreigners on its external liabilities, because the U.S. holds more foreign risky assets while foreign investors hold more U.S. safe assets. This asymmetry in portfolio returns has been referred to as theexorbitant privilegeof the U.S. (Gourinchas and Rey (2022)). The dashed line in Figure1depicts the cumulative net capital flows to the U.S. net of any valuation gains. The difference between the solid and dashed lines therefore measures how the return differential affects U.S. external positions.
  From 2002 to 2010, the U.S. did indeed earn an exorbitant privilege—a positive return differential between U.S. external assets and liabilities allowed the U.S. NFA position to decline by less than the cumulative amount of net capital flows. From 2010 to 2019, however, this return differential reversed. As a result of this reversal in the return differential, the gap between the U.S. NFA position and cumulative net capital flows vanished. The bottom panel of Figure1further illustrates this point by plotting the percent difference between the net portfolio position and cumulative net flows, along with a smoothed trend line.
  What are the key drivers of global imbalances? Why did the U.S. external position decline over the past two decades? What were the sources of the reversal in the U.S. exorbitant privilege after 2010? To answer these questions, we use a portfolio-based demand system approach to decompose both the level of and the returns on the U.S. external portfolio position. Our approach allows us to attribute drivers of the U.S. external portfolio positions to various factors proposed in the literature, such as changes in investor savings and asset issuances, monetary policies, changes in asset quality, and investor demand.
  We present three key findings. First, countervailing forces underlie the seemingly uniform widening of the U.S. NFA position.3While the supply of global savings and issuances and monetary policies contribute to the widening of the U.S. NFA position, shifts in investor demand partially offset this trend. As a result, any theory that seeks to explain the decline in the U.S. portfolio imbalance using just one of these channels alone is unlikely to succeed quantitatively. Second, we show that a shift in investor demand toward U.S. equities increased realized returns on U.S. external liabilities and was primarily responsible for the reversal of privilege.
  Our third key finding showcases a novel quantity dimension of exorbitant privilege for U.S. debt issuers. Previous analyses of U.S. exorbitant privilege focus on prices and the relatively low returns earned on U.S. liabilities. By contrast, we show that the U.S. can issue a substantially larger quantity of long-term debt for a given increase in interest rate, relative to other developed economies. Interestingly, this ranking almost completely reverses when we scale issuance by each country's GDP. This quantity dimension of exorbitant privilege is crucial for fully characterizing the benefits earned by the U.S. through its unique position in the financial system.
  Our approach builds on the demand system approach to asset pricing (Koijen and Yogo (2019,2020), Koijen, Richmond, and Yogo (2020)). We adopt the demand system of Koijen and Yogo (2020) with important modifications to wealth dynamics that allow us to decompose the drivers of global imbalances. We model and estimate investors' asset demand curves as functions of observed and unobserved (latent) asset characteristics. Investors are able to substitute across countries and across asset classes. In equilibrium, investor countries and central banks must hold the total quantity of assets outstanding.
  We estimate our framework using a comprehensive data set of bilateral equity and debt portfolio positions. Using data from multiple sources, we improve the quality of the standard databases along several important dimensions. Our data set covers both private and public sector holdings, which allow us to assess the effect of asset purchases by the U.S. Federal Reserve and specific foreign central banks. We verify that the U.S. NFA implied from these portfolio positions closely traces out the aggregate data, and that our data capture cross-country holdings that are traditionally poorly represented due to indirect holdings through tax havens (Coppola et al. (2021)). We estimate our model by exploiting exogenous variation in investor portfolios driven by exogenous variation in asset characteristics across countries.4
  This asset demand system allows us to evaluate how asset prices and investor portfolio holdings change in response to three sets of variables: (i) investors' savings and asset issuances, (ii) central banks' monetary policies, and (iii) changes in asset characteristics and in investors' latent demand. We show how these variables map to the classic balance of payments decompositions as well as how they correspond to existing explanations for global imbalances and asymmetries that have been proposed in the literature.
  We treat these variables as exogenous in our framework, and they jointly explain the endogenous asset prices, exchange rates, and investors' wealth and portfolio allocations. For each year, had all of the exogenous variables remained unchanged, the endogenous variables, including asset prices and portfolio holdings, would have remained the same. By iteratively restoring the changes in exogenous variables from the previous year to the current year, and recomputing equilibrium asset prices and portfolio choices along the way, we are able to attribute variations in the level of and the return on the U.S. NFA to different drivers.
  We begin by measuring the effects of global private savings in excess of domestic investment opportunities. This group of variables broadly maps to savings glut explanations for global imbalances. These savings and issuance variables contribute to a 7.6% widening of the U.S. NFA per annum. In comparison, the actual U.S. NFA widened by 9.4% per annum in our sample from 2002 to 2019. In other words, the pattern of global private savings and asset issuances can account for about 80% of the trend in U.S. portfolio imbalances. We further differentiate the contributions from different regions and find that asset issuances in the U.S. and foreign savings from European and Asian-Pacific developed economies are the predominant drivers.
  We next measure the effects of central bank holdings and monetary policy rates. We find that these monetary policy variables further widen the U.S. NFA by 9.9% per annum. Foreign central banks' reserve purchases of U.S. assets are responsible for about half of this widening, with policy rates driving the other half. We further disaggregate the contribution of foreign central bank reserve policy by employing data on the currency composition of central bank reserves. Consistent with explanations of emerging market savings being channeled through central bank reserves, we find that emerging market reserves are a salient source of the decline in U.S. NFA, contributing 4.3% per year versus 0.2% per year for developed market central banks.
  Finally, and perhaps most surprisingly, we show that changes in asset characteristics and investor demand partially offset these trends, driving a reversal of 8.2% per annum in the U.S. NFA growth. These drivers broadly map to explanations for global imbalances based on shifts in asset quality and investor demand, but are not directly observable in the aggregate time series because they are overshadowed by the effects of savings and issuances as well as monetary policies. As a result, when we consider all three sets of drivers together, the U.S. external portfolio imbalance becomes substantially more negative, but this decline would have been much greater if asset (country) characteristics and investor demand had remained constant. We also investigate specific components of changes in investor demand and find that its contribution is driven primarily by (i) a market-wide shift from debt assets to equity assets, which impacts the U.S. external imbalance because U.S. debt is widely held by foreigners, and (ii) a decline of the appeal of U.S. debt assets in terms of their observable and latent characteristics.
  Having explained the trend in the U.S. NFA, we next decompose the main drivers of the valuation effects on the U.S. external portfolio. It is well known that, historically, the U.S. has earned a large positive excess return on its external portfolio. From 2002 to 2010, these valuation effects helped reduce the U.S. NFA position by 18.3% per annum. Perhaps less well known is the fact that this trend completely reverses after 2010. Between 2010 and 2019, valuation effects actually widen the U.S. NFA by an average of 6.9% per annum. We use our model to shed new light on which economic forces explain this reversal in valuation effects.
  We show that the overwhelming share of the trends in realized returns can be explained largely by an increase in investor demand for U.S. equities post-2010. Increasing demand for U.S. equities relative to equities of other countries raised the realized returns on U.S. external liabilities and explain the decline in the net valuation effects on the U.S. NFA.
  In the final section of the paper, we explore how variation in global investor demand for debt from different countries and downward-sloping demand curves influence a novel notion of privilege. Taking demand curves and portfolios as given in 2019, we estimate the quantity of new long-term debt that can be issued by residents of each of the G10 countries until their yield increases by 1%. We find that, relative to all other G10 countries, U.S. residents could issue approximately three to five times the amount of long-term debt before its yield increases by 1%. These findings help further quantify a notion of exorbitant privilege enjoyed by the U.S.—the ability to borrow large quantities at low rates.
  Literature Review. Our paper contributes to a large literature that studies the drivers of NFA dynamics and the composition of global portfolios (Gourinchas and Rey (2007a,2007b), Lane and Milesi-Ferretti (2007), Curcuru, Dvorak, and Warnock (2008)). In particular, our paper focuses on the imbalances in the U.S. NFA position (Caballero, Farhi, and Gourinchas (2008), Gourinchas and Rey (2022), Gourinchas and Rey (2014)). We contribute to this literature by taking a financial perspective and model the NFA position as the outcome of a portfolio decision. A number of related papers study portfolio models and their theoretical implications for international imbalances (Ghironi, Lee, and Rebucci (2015), Devereux and Sutherland (2009), Cova, Pisani, and Rebucci (2009), Tille and Van Wincoop (2010)). By contrast, we directly estimate a flexible portfolio choice model that matches observed portfolio holdings and permits a broad decomposition of the drivers of the U.S. external portfolio.
  A key contribution to the global imbalances literature shows that the U.S. benefited from an exorbitant privilege in asset returns. From 1952 to 2004, the U.S. external imbalance was partially reduced by a positive difference between the returns on its external asset and liability positions (Gourinchas and Rey (2007a)). Atkeson, Heathcote, and Perri (2021) uncover and study changes in these return dynamics, and attribute these changes to high returns on U.S. equities driven by mark-ups and profits. Our paper contributes to this literature by also highlighting the reversal in U.S. privilege and decomposing sources of variation in the returns on the U.S. external portfolio. In addition, we contribute to the literature on exorbitant privilege by measuring a novel quantity dimension of U.S. privilege.
  A large literature studies drivers of capital flows to and from countries. Drivers of capital flows in the previous literature include institutional quality (Alfaro, Kalemli-Ozcan, and Volosovych (2008)), demographic factors (Lane and Milesi-Ferretti (2001), Carvalho, Ferrero, and Nechio (2016)), financial development (Caballero, Farhi, and Gourinchas (2008)), oil shocks (Kilian, Rebucci, and Spatafora (2009)), and interactions between financial frictions and international trade (Antras and Caballero (2009)).5Our contribution is to evaluate the relative importance of different channels in driving flows in a unified framework. This allows us to conclude that the U.S. NFA dynamics are likely driven by multiple factors that partially offset each other.
  Finally, the methodology we use in this paper builds on a literature that explicitly measures asset demand elasticities to understand changes in asset prices (Krishnamurthy and Vissing-Jorgensen (2012), Koijen and Yogo (2020), Koijen, Richmond, and Yogo (2020), Koijen et al. (2021)).6Most closely related to our work is Koijen and Yogo (2020), who develop the demand system for international financial assets that we employ. A primary application of the demand system in their paper is to provide a variance decomposition of exchange rates and asset prices globally. In contrast, our paper uses the demand system approach to study the drivers of portfolio holdings and the U.S. external imbalance. We further extend the setup in Koijen and Yogo (2020) to study the joint dynamics of flows, returns, and portfolios. In Jiang, Richmond, and Zhang (2022), we apply a similar approach to study the drivers of the U.S. dollar exchange rate.
  This paper proceeds as follows. SectionIprovides a theoretical framework for connecting the NFA dynamics to portfolio choices and market clearing. SectionIalso relates the components of the framework to the literature on global imbalances. SectionIIspecifies our model in detail. SectionIIIdescribes data sources and summary statistics, discusses the estimation procedure and identification strategy, and presents the estimation results. SectionIVpresents our empirical results. SectionVconcludes.
article.I. Model Overview:
In this section, we provide a broad overview of our model of international asset markets. Our approach is to understand NFA dynamics from the perspective of portfolio allocation. After laying out the key equations in our portfolio choice model, we show how the model's components relate to the standard balance-of-payments identity.
  Time is discrete. There areN$N$issuer countries in the world that issue assets, andI$I$investor countries that contain representative investors who allocate their wealth across the asset space. These two sets of countries can overlap. We allow investors' wealth to respond endogenously to portfolio choices and asset revaluation over time, which is crucial for our application of the demand system in the context of international portfolio dynamics.
  Having expressed the U.S. NFA dynamics as a portfolio problem, we can study its variation using asset pricing tools. Our approach allows us to decompose its dynamics into the following components, which we discuss in the context of existing views on causes of the widening U.S. NFA position.
  First, asavings glutview argues that foreign savings in excess of their domestic investment opportunities contribute to flows into the U.S. debt market (Bernanke (2005)). One source of the savings glut is the strong savings motive of developed countries with aging populations and inequality (Rachel and Smith (2018), Mian, Straub, and Sufi (2020), Auclert et al. (2021)). Another source is emerging countries with high growth and willingness to save in the developed markets, in particular in the U.S. (Caballero (2006), Caballero, Farhi, and Gourinchas (2008)).
  To capture these forces, we group investor savings and asset issuances into the first block of variables we examine. These variables are measured by an investor countryi$i$’s net financial savings,Fi,t$F_{i, t}$, in excess of that country's asset issuance,ΔQt(i)$\Delta Q_t(i)$. Intuitively, these excess savings have to be allocated to a foreign country such as the U.S. that, according to the market-clearing equation (3), will contribute to higher asset prices in the U.S. as well as net inflows of capital into the U.S. This excess saving is also closely connected to the trade balance, which, as we have shown, is equal to the net savings minus the net asset issuance. We consider each country's consumption and export decisions as exogenous, and trace out how the excess savings and the corresponding trade balance drive movements in asset prices and capital flows.
  Second, central banks' monetary policies could play an important role in shaping global imbalances. We consider two types of monetary policies: (i) reserve accumulation and (ii) changes in nominal short-term interest rates. Reserve accumulation includes both official reserve holdings (Bernanke (2005), Farhi, Gourinchas, and Rey (2011)) and quantitative easing (QE) (Krishnamurthy and Vissing-Jorgensen (2013), Koijen et al. (2017), Krishnamurthy, Nagel, and Vissing-Jorgensen (2018), Acharya and Krishnamurthy (2018)). To capture these central bank holdings, we differentiate private and official holdings of assets in our framework. When foreign or U.S. official holdings of U.S. debt assets increase, U.S. asset valuations also increase via market clearing (equation (3)).
  Interest rate policies could also affect U.S. external imbalances (Ahmed et al. (2018), Jiang, Krishnamurthy, and Lustig (2020)). To understand the impact of changes in the short-term interest rate, our framework differentiates between investment in equity, long-term debt, and short-term debt as separate asset classes. We model the nominal short-term interest rate as an exogenous policy outcome for each country that impacts the demand for short-term debt.
  Third, global imbalances are driven by changes to asset quality and investor demand. A prominent type of taste shift is flight-to-safety (Blanchard, Giavazzi, and Sa (2005), Krishnamurthy and Vissing-Jorgensen (2012), Caballero, Farhi, and Gourinchas (2017), Maggiori (2017), Jiang, Krishnamurthy, and Lustig (2021), Jiang (2021)). Under this view, foreign investors may want to hold their excess savings in safer assets. As the supply of safe assets is limited and concentrated in a small number of advanced economies, foreign investors have a strong desire to purchase assets in these destinations—especially during periods of financial turmoil. More generally, changes in risk appetite, investor taste, and asset characteristics themselves (Atkeson, Heathcote, and Perri (2021)) can shift investors' portfolio positions across asset classes and across countries. Koijen and Yogo (2020), Gabaix and Koijen (2021), Bacchetta, Davenport, and Van Wincoop (2022), and Bacchetta, Van Wincoop, and Young (2023) show that these shifts in investor demand account for a large fraction of variation in portfolio flows and asset returns.
  Our framework captures shifts in investor demand by modeling investor preferences for specific asset characteristics (e.g., country size or domestic inflation) as well as exogenous demand shifts both within and across asset classes. Over time, a country's characteristics can change and make its financial assets more desirable to international investors. We thus differentiate between increases in investor demand for assets that originate from increases in the supply of savings and increases in investor demand for assets with particular features.
article.II. Empirical Portfolio Choice Model:
In the following section, we fill in the details of our model. Our empirical approach uses the demand system approach to asset pricing (Koijen and Yogo (2019,2020), Koijen, Richmond, and Yogo (2020)). Our specification of international portfolio demand curves follows Koijen and Yogo (2020), while the wealth dynamics are modified in our setting in order to study the dynamics of international portfolios, their returns, and investor savings.
  To operationalize the portfolio-based framework, we need a realistic specification of portfolio weights. We first introduce asset classes indexed byℓ$\ell$: short-term debt (ℓ=1$\ell =1$), long-term debt (ℓ=2$\ell =2$), and equity (ℓ=3$\ell =3$). We use the pair(n,ℓ)$(n,\ell)$to denote issuer countryn$n$’s asset of asset classℓ$\ell$. For example, we denote investor countryi$i$’s portfolio weight for this asset bywi,t(n,ℓ)$w_{i,t}(n, \ell)$. Moreover, each asset class containsN+1$N + 1$assets—one for each issuer country and an “outside” asset indexed byn=0$n = 0$. This outside asset contains investors' holdings in small countries that are not in our main sample of issuer countries due to data availability limitations.
  This desirability term has three components. First,μi,t(n,ℓ)$\mu _{i,t}(n,\ell)$denotes the expected return at timet$t$for countryi$i$’s investor in countryn$n$’s asset of classℓ$\ell$, which we measure using the combination of market-to-book ratios and exchange rates that best predicts future returns—details of this regression are in the following section. The second component is a set of observable asset characteristicsxi,t(n)${\bf x}_{i, t}(n)$that can be country-specific or bilateral in nature. The loadings,θℓ${\bm{\theta }}_\ell$, capture the weight that investors place on the characteristics within each asset class. The third component,κi,t(n,ℓ)$\kappa _{i, t}(n, \ell)$, is referred to as latent demand and describes additional variation in the portfolio weights that is not captured by the expected return or observed asset characteristics.
  For concreteness, consider the U.S. representative investor deciding on her portfolio weight on German long-term debt. Thus,i$i$is the U.S.,n$n$is Germany, andℓ=2$\ell =2$represents long-term debt. In this case,μi,t(n,ℓ)$\mu _{i, t}(n, \ell)$captures the local-currency return that the U.S. investor expects to earn on German long-term debt,xi,t(n)${\bf x}_{i, t}(n)$captures characteristics such as the size (GDP) of Germany and the geographic distance between the U.S. and Germany, andθℓ${\bm{\theta }}_\ell$captures the extent to which these characteristics matter for the long-term bond portfolio allocation. By assumption, the importance of asset characteristics to the portfolio allocation is the same across investors within an asset class.
  We differentiate between asset demand by private investors and asset demand by central banks. We useBi,t(n,ℓ)$B_{i,t}(n,\ell)$to denote the quantity of countryn$n$’s assets held by countryi$i$’s central bank, which we take as exogenous.10The units for these central bank holdings are book value in local currency.
  LetQt(n,ℓ)$Q_t(n,\ell)$denote the book quantity supplied by countryn$n$in asset classℓ$\ell$in its local currency. In particular,Qt(n,ℓ)$Q_t(n, \ell)$is the total book value in local currency for equity, and the par value in local currency for long-term and short-term debt. We assume that the quantity of assets outstanding in each period is exogenously determined. Nevertheless, the dollar book value,Et(n)Qt(n,ℓ)$E_t(n) Q_t(n,\ell)$, and the dollar market value,PBt(n,ℓ)Et(n)Qt(n,ℓ)$PB_t(n,\ell) E_t(n) Q_t(n,\ell)$, of any asset are endogenous, because exchange rates and market-to-book ratios are endogenously determined.
  There are three asset classes withN$N$assets each, which leads to3N$3N$market-clearing conditions. Taking short-term bond prices as given, there areN$N$long-term bond prices,N$N$equity prices, andN−1$N - 1$exchange rates with respect to the dollar. Following Koijen and Yogo (2020), we assume that the Federal Reserve adjusts the supply of U.S. short-term debt to clear markets. This assumption leads to an exactly determined system in theN$N$long-term bond prices,N$N$equity prices,N−1$N-1$exchange rates, and the U.S. short-term debt supply.12We use this system in the following section to study how various components have driven variation in the U.S. NFA position.
article.III. Model Estimation:
We rely on three types of data: (i) cross-country bilateral portfolio holdings, (ii) asset/country characteristics, and (iii) realized asset returns. At each stage of our data construction, we combine the best available data to get an accurate representation of cross-border holdings and asset returns while paying special attention to the U.S. NFA position and U.S. portfolio returns. We summarize our data here and relegate the details and data sources to SectionIIIof theInternet Appendix.
  Compared with existing studies of international capital flows, we improve the quality of data on cross-border holdings and returns in three ways. First, we use the reallocation matrices of Coppola et al. (2021) to account for misattributed investments in offshore financial centers. Second, we estimate the reserve holdings of specific central banks to disaggregate the quantities attributed to official asset purchases at the country and region levels. Third, we use detailed estimates of asset returns from the Treasury International Capital data to construct reliable estimates of capital gains and net savings.
  Our resulting sample ranges from 2002 to 2019, and consists of 35 investor countries and 33 issuer countries for which we have comprehensive holdings and characteristics. TableIA.VIIpresents the specific countries in our sample and their classifications. TableIA.VIIIlists the central banks for which we are able to construct bilateral holdings. Holdings in issuer countries for which we do not observe a complete panel of characteristics and asset price data are aggregated into a single “outside” country. Investments in the outside country comprise 3.5% of all observed holdings in our data.
  TableIpresents the top five bilateral positions in the U.S. assets and liabilities for each asset class in 2002 and 2019, as well as the total positions for all other foreign countries. On the liability side, the largest long-term U.S. debt liabilities in 2019 are held by the Fed, the European Union, and China's central bank. For equity, the largest liabilities are held by the European Union and the United Kingdom. On the asset side, China, the United Kingdom, and Japan hold the largest equity positions in 2019. Importantly, these bilateral positions are consistent with those found in Coppola et al. (2021).
  In Figure2, we present the time series of central bank holdings of U.S. securities by region. This figure shows that a large portion of central bank holdings can be attributed to the Fed's QE after the global financial crisis. Outside of the Fed's holdings, emerging markets have the largest central bank holdings of U.S. securities, followed by developed markets.
  In addition to the holdings data, we build a panel of asset characteristics to construct the vectorxi,t(n)${\bf x}_{i,t}(n)$. We choose a set of characteristics that investors likely use to proxy for expected returns and the riskiness of assets. These characteristics include asset-level characteristics such as the total market-to-book value of equity and the yields on short-term and long-term debt. We use the yield on three-month government debt to capture the yield on short-term debt, and we use the yield on 10-year government debt to capture the yield on long-term debt. We also observe country-level characteristics that may affect the risk profile for all assets in a country. These country-level characteristics include proxies for country size and development (GDP, population), trade network centrality (Richmond (2019), Lustig and Richmond (2020), Jiang and Richmond (2023)), sovereign default risk, and market volatility. Finally, we include a standard set of macroeconomic characteristics: real exchange rates, inflation, bilateral export shares, bilateral import shares, and the physical distance between countries.
  The main challenge to consistently estimating equations (14) and (15) is that expected returns may be endogenous to the latent demand of investors,κi,t(n,ℓ)$\kappa _{i,t}(n,\ell)$andξi,t(ℓ)$\xi _{i,t}(\ell)$. Consider the estimation of the within-asset-class demand curves, equation (14). If investors have high latent demand for a specific issuer's asset, the price of this asset will be higher, which will impact the asset's expected return and bias the estimated demand coefficientβℓ$\beta _\ell$due to the correlation between the regressor,μi,t(n,ℓ)$\mu _{i,t}(n,\ell)$, and the residual,κi,t(n,ℓ)$\kappa _{i,t}(n,\ell)$. A similar argument applies to the estimation of the across-asset-demand curves in equation (15). If a particular asset class has high latent demand, this will increase the price of this asset class and potentially bias the estimation since the inclusive value,1+∑n=1Nδi,t(n,ℓ)$1+\sum _{n=1}^N \delta _{i,t}(n,\ell)$, contains the price. To address these endogeneity concerns, we construct instruments for both estimation equations, building on the identification strategy in Koijen and Yogo (2020).
  Our goal is to estimate cross-sectional demand elasticities, which are determined by the coefficients in equations (14) and (15). We therefore require instruments that are correlated with cross-sectional differences in country-level expected returns for equation (14) and asset-class–level desirabilities for equation (15). We do not necessarily require that our instruments vary over time.
  We identify exogenous cross-sectional variation in prices and exchange rates to use as instruments for expected returns. To obtain such variation, we use our model to construct instruments for prices and exchange rates based on cross-sectional differences in characteristics of different countries. The market-clearing condition (13) implies that asset prices are higher for assets that have higher weights in portfolios of investors with more wealth. Our baseline instrument construction therefore captures exogenous variation in portfolio weights by projecting portfolio weights onto characteristics that are exogenous to latent demand (e.g., geographic characteristics). Then, taking the country-level asset supply and the wealth distribution as given, we compute counterfactual prices using the market-clearing condition (13). This procedure ultimately produces a set of implied prices and exchange rates that are determined solely by the exogenous characteristics of all of the countries, which we use as instruments.
  Under our baseline assumption, investor wealth and asset supply are exogenous, which is in line with assumptions made for endowment economies. We think of aggregate investor wealth at the country level as being largely determined by historical sources of economic development and size, which were set prior to the determination of the current cross-section of portfolio holdings and latent demand. To address the concerns that may arise about our baseline estimation procedure, we present several variations of our estimation that relax different parts of our identifying assumption (16) in theAppendix. Important variations include allowing asset characteristics such as GDP to be endogenously determined, and using exogenous variation in asset supply and investor wealth when constructing the instrument. In SectionIV.E, we show that our key results are quantitatively robust to these specific variations. We also show that our key results are robust to using a range of demand elasticities that is consistent with those found in the literature.
  After we construct these instruments based on the model structure, we confirm that they are correlated with expected returns and are therefore relevant and strong. This strong first stage further validates our instrument construction strategy.
  Due to the limited availability of data, we estimate all regressions over the full sample to ensure that our estimates are precise. While in theory it would be possible to estimate the demand equations period-by-period with a larger sample of international holdings data, we are constrained by the number of countries for which we observe complete data.
  The results of estimating equation (17) are reported in TableIA.II. Investors tend to have higher portfolio weights in countries that are larger and geographically closer. For all asset classes there is a large home bias in portfolio holdings. These characteristics explain a substantial amount of the variation in bilateral portfolio weights, with theR2$R^2$ranging from 49% to 67% across the three asset classes. Furthermore, these characteristics explain a substantial share of the within-investor country variation in portfolio weights, with a withinR2$R^2$of approximately 20%. We compute the predicted values from these regressions, which we refer to as exogenous asset desirabilities,δ̂i,t(n,ℓ)$\hat{\delta }_{i,t}(n,\ell)$. These desirabilities are driven entirely by variation in these exogenous characteristics.
  The results for estimating equation (15) are reported in TableIA.III.14The first thing to note is that the first-stageF-statistics in the bottom three rows of the table are all greater than 100 (Stock and Yogo (2002)). These high first-stageF-statistics imply that the instruments for the inclusive value are all highly correlated with the asset-class–level desirabilities, even though they are constructed entirely from exogenous asset characteristics. Next, allλℓ$\lambda _\ell$values are between zero and one. This implies that there is some substitution between asset classes when the relative value of an asset class varies. This is in contrast to the caseλℓ=0$\lambda _\ell =0$, in which the allocations across asset classes are independent of the relative desirabilities of individual assets. Whenλℓ=1$\lambda _\ell =1$, the substitution between asset classes depends only on the desirabilities of individual issuer countries' assets, and the demand system collapses to one tier. Our estimates are between these two polar cases, implying that there is some segmentation across asset classes.15
  Intuitively, the above procedure identifies differences in expected returns that arise due to the fact that asset prices are higher in countries that are geographically closer to large investor countries and in countries that tend to issue fewer assets. In this way, we obtain instruments for exchange rates and asset prices that we use next to identify the within-asset-class demand curve.
  We also note that the characteristics of all countries enter into these model-implied portfolio weights, which is the result of the term1+∑k=1Nδ̂i,t(k,ℓ)$1+\sum _{k=1}^N \hat{\delta }_{i,t}(k,\ell)$summing over all of the exogenous desirabilities. As a result, our instruments are nonlinear functions of all issuer country characteristics, rather than just those of a specific issuer country only. Therefore, our instruments still remain valid, despite the fact that we control for each issuer country's characteristics in the estimation of investor countries' demand curves. This identification approach is in the spirit of a large literature in industrial organization that uses the characteristics of competing products to instrument for a given product's price (Reiss and Wolak (2007), Gandhi and Houde (2020)).
  With our instruments in hand for asset-class desirabilities and for expected returns, we estimate the regression equation (14). The issuer country characteristics are log GDP, log population, trade network centrality, sovereign default risk, volatility, real exchange rate, and inflation. Bilateral characteristics are import and export exposures and distance. We also include indicator variables for domestic investment, U.S. issuer, investor country, and year fixed effects. For short-term debt, we instrument expected returns withÊt(n)$\hat{E}_t(n)$. For long-term debt and equity, we instrument expected returns withÊt(n)$\hat{E}_t(n)$andP̂t(n,ℓ)$\hat{P}_t(n,\ell)$forℓ=2,3$\ell = 2, 3$.
  The baseline estimates for within-asset-class demand curves are presented in TableIA.V.17The coefficients on expected returns are all positive, which implies that conditional on our set of asset characteristics, assets with higher expected returns are preferred by investors. The coefficients on asset characteristics are all intuitive. Investors prefer assets that provide better hedges against systematic risks, such as the assets of larger countries (higher GDP). Conditional on countries having higher GDP, investors prefer countries with lower population, which implies that they tend to prefer countries with higher GDP per capita. Investors also prefer assets from countries that are closer and with whom they have a stronger trade relationship. Finally, the next-to-last row of TableIA.Vshows that there is strong home bias in all asset classes.
  Our estimates imply average demand elasticities of 229 for short-term debt, 2.0 for long-term debt, and 1.8 for equities.18These numbers are comparable to those found in Koijen and Yogo (2020), which we would expect since we employ a variation of their estimation methodology. For short-term debt with a maturity of three months, this elasticity implies that a 1% increase in annualized yield increases demand for short-term debt by 58%. For long-term debt with a maturity of 10 years, this demand elasticity implies that a 1% increase in annualized yield increases demand for long-term debt by 20%.
  To ensure the robustness of our findings, we present estimates from a number of alternative identification and estimation procedures in theAppendix. While different estimation procedures lead to somewhat different point estimates, the elasticities do not change substantively. Most importantly, we show that our key results are robust to these variations. Finally, beyond studying these specific estimation variations, we also show that our results are robust to using a range of demand elasticities that is consistent with those found in the literature.
article.IV. Decomposing the U.S. NFA Position:
In this section, we use our model to decompose and explain the trends in the U.S. NFA position over the past 20 years. We begin by describing our decomposition methodology that attributes the changes in the U.S. NFA position to primitive variables. We then describe the specific sequence of steps in our decomposition exercise. Finally, we present our results.
  To decompose changes in the U.S. NFA position in yeart$t$, we begin by setting all primitive exogenous variables in our model back to their values in the previous yeart−1$t-1$. We compute the equilibrium NFA position using the market-clearing condition, and refer to this equilibrium as thebaselinestep. We then sequentially restore the primitive variables to their actual year-t$t$values and recompute equilibrium asset prices and portfolio holdings at each stage. After restoring all variables, we arrive at the actual observed year-t$t$NFA in the data, which we refer to as theobservedstep.
  More concretely, consider the sequence ofJ+1$J + 1$steps starting with the baseline step 0 and ending with the observed stepJ$J$. In each step, we turn one primitive variable from its value in year(t−1)$(t-1)$to its value in yeart$t$, and recompute the equilibrium. LetNFA∼US,tj$\widetilde{NFA}^{j}_{US, t}$denote the implied U.S. NFA position in stepj$j$. By construction, in step 0,NFA∼US,t0$\widetilde{NFA}^{0}_{US, t}$is equal to the actual U.S. NFA in the previous year,NFAUS,t−1$NFA_{US, t-1}$. In stepJ$J$, all primitive variables take their actual values in yeart$t$, andNFA∼US,tJ$\widetilde{NFA}^{J}_{US, t}$is equal to the actual U.S. NFA in the current year,NFAUS,t$NFA_{US, t}$.
  Having specified our decomposition framework, we now describe the sequence ofJ$J$steps we take in our exercise. As we discuss in SectionI.C, our choice of the primitive variables are inspired by various literatures that study the global imbalances from different perspectives. In particular, these variables should represent (i) investor savings and asset issuances, (ii) monetary policies, and (iii) shifts in investor demand and asset characteristics.
  We start by measuring the contribution of investors' net savings,Fi,t$F_{i,t}$, and asset issuances,Qt(n,ℓ)$Q_{t}(n,\ell)$, in various geographic regions. In each step, we restore investors' savings and issuances simultaneously for a given geographic region.20We also restore changes in book-per-share,St(n,ℓ)${S}_{t}\operatorname{(}n,\ell )$, in this step. In doing so, our exercise allows us evaluate the effects of the private savings glut, which is driven by an excess of foreign savings that are not satiated by local investment opportunities. More precisely, we first restore the savings and issuances in the U.S., then in the developed markets in the Asia Pacific region, then in the developed markets in the Europe region, and finally in the remaining developed and emerging markets.
  Next, we account for the various forms of monetary policies. We start by restoring the changes in the U.S. Federal Reserve holdings of domestic debt assets via QE. We then restore the portfolio changes in foreign central bank currency reserves, first for developed markets and subsequently for emerging markets. Having restored these quantities, we turn to the monetary policy rates by first restoring the U.S. short-term interest rate and then foreign short-term interest rates.
  Finally, we restore the changes in country characteristicsxi,t(n)${\bf x}_{i, t}(n)$, within-asset-class latent demandκi,t(n,ℓ)$\kappa _{i, t}(n,\ell)$, and cross-asset-class latent demandξi,t(ℓ)$\xi _{i, t}(\ell)$. These steps account for changes in the relative desirability of assets that arise from changes in asset fundamentals (such as economic growth), as well as changes in the desirability of assets and asset classes that are not captured by observed characteristics. Taken together, these steps restore all variables and fully account for the realized changes in the U.S. NFA position.
  TableIIreports the decomposition results for the overall trend in the U.S. NFA position. The first column reports the average contributionΔ¯j$\overline{\Delta }_j$of each set of primitive variables to the log change of the U.S. NFA in the full sample. The second and third columns report the separate contributions of the flow and valuation effects.
  Looking first at the bottom row of TableII, we see that the U.S. NFA position widened (i.e., became more negative) by an average of 9.4% each year in our sample period. This decline in the U.S. NFA position represents a dramatic increase in the value of U.S. external liabilities relative to the value of U.S. external assets. The second and third columns show that the decline in the U.S. NFA position was partially mitigated by valuation effects. Without accounting for the changes in asset prices and exchange rates, flows in the quantity of capital would have resulted in an even larger widening in the U.S. NFA position of 13.3% per year. However, these capital flows coincided with lower returns on the U.S. external liabilities relative to the U.S. external assets, and these valuation effects helped dampen the widening by an average of 5.0% per year.
  Each row in TableIIdecomposes this average 9.4% per year widening in the U.S. NFA position into the various blocks of primitive variables that we discuss above. Within each block, the bottom row reports the average contribution of the entire block. The main blocks are savings and issuances, monetary policies (which we further break down into central bank reserves and interest rates), and demand shifts and asset characteristics.
  The main takeaway from these rows is that the simple downward trend in the U.S. NFA position is driven by multiple countervailing forces, as opposed to by a single factor. First, accounting for savings and issuances around the world explains a widening in the U.S. NFA of 7.6% per year. Accounting for changes in central bank reserves and monetary policy rates around the world contributes another 9.9% per year to the average widening in the U.S. NFA position. The effects of these forces vastly overshoot the actual trend in the data. The changes in the asset characteristics and investor demand shifts partially reverse the trend in the U.S. NFA, offsetting its growth by 8.2% per year.
  The implication of TableIIis that any theory that relies primarily on a single explanation for the change in the U.S. NFA position is unlikely to succeed quantitatively. Instead, theories explaining the decline in the U.S. NFA position need to account for the interaction of multiple narratives. In the rest of this section, we discuss each block of primitive variables in greater detail.
  The first block in TableIIdescribes the contributions of countries' savings and issuances. A country's savings and issuances affect its NFA position through two channels. First, as a country issues more assets, the price of its assets decline, foreign investors tend to purchase more of its assets, and capital flows into the country on net. Second, as an investor country saves more, domestic capital flows toward foreign issuer countries based on the investor country's existing portfolio weights. If the investor country allocates much of its savings to domestic assets, then its savings will also result in an appreciation of the domestic assets.
  Figure3depicts the cumulative net savings flows to/from the U.S. by different regions. Savings flows to the U.S. from each region are computed by multiplying the investors' portfolio allocations in the previous period with their savings flows. Savings from the U.S. to each foreign region are computed in the same manner. Net savings flows are the difference between these two flows. Over our sample, developed Europe was the largest net saver in the U.S., with this particular measure having an average value of $220 billion per year. Developed Asia had average flows of $57 billion to the U.S. per year and countries in our other group had average net outflows from the U.S. of $33 billion per year.
  Turning to the contribution of these savings flows and issuances to the U.S. NFA, we find that U.S. savings and issuances explain a 6.4% growth in the U.S. NFA per year. For the U.S. this effect is driven mainly by issuances. As the U.S. issues more equity and debt assets, part of these new issuances are purchased by foreign investors, leading to an increase in U.S. external liabilities. The magnitude of capital flows to the U.S. is even more stark when we hold asset prices constant, as suggested by the flow effects in column (2) of TableII. In column (3), the valuation effects partially offset the changes in quantities, as the U.S. issuance also depresses the valuation of its foreign liabilities.
  The savings and issuances from the Asia Pacific and Europe developed markets lead to further widening of the U.S. NFA position, by 2.4% and 2.1% per year, respectively. This effect is mainly driven by increases in Asian Pacific and European savings. As foreign investors accumulate savings, a substantial share of these savings is allocated to U.S. financial assets due to the characteristics of the U.S. that make it a desirable destination for foreign capital as measured by the estimated demand curves. This leads to net capital inflows to the U.S. (i.e., a positive flow component) and a higher return on U.S. assets (i.e., a positive valuation component). While non-U.S. issuances of debt and equity assets can potentially attract capital away from the U.S., this countervailing pull of foreign issuances on U.S. savings is relatively weak.
  Finally, foreign savings and issuances in the other markets lead to a narrowing of the U.S. NFA positions. This is at first surprising, since countries like China and India are known to invest in U.S. asset markets. That said, much of the financial savings in these developing countries are invested via their central banks instead of by their private investors. As a result, the effects of their aggregate savings remain to be seen in our next block that focuses on central bank reserve holdings.
  Overall, our analysis of global issuances and savings relates most closely with the literature on the global savings glut, which studies large increases in global savings that seek foreign investment opportunities. Our decomposition reveals that this effect on the U.S. NFA is driven mainly by a combination of the developed economies' investment needs and U.S. issuances.
  We next examine the second block in TableII, which captures the effects of central bank reserves on the U.S. NFA. U.S. QE takes part of the supply of the U.S. long-term debt out of the market and pushes up its price. As a result, this operation squeezes out foreign holdings of U.S. issued liabilities in quantities, but increases the value of the remaining foreign liabilities. Consistent with this intuition, U.S. QE explains a narrowing in the NFA position in terms of flows, but a widening in the NFA position due to valuation effects. The two effects roughly cancel out.
  In the next two rows of TableII, we find that emerging market central banks (such as China) play a major role in widening the U.S. NFA, as foreign reserve purchases of U.S. long-term debt increase U.S. external liabilities both in quantities and in value. By comparison, the central banks in developed markets play a much smaller role. Overall, the role of central bank reserves on the widening U.S. NFA is explained by reserve holdings in emerging markets.
  The third block in TableIIexplains the effects of monetary policy rates. Changes in U.S. monetary policy rates tend to slightly widen the U.S. NFA position. By inspecting these effects year by year, we find that an increase in the U.S. short-term interest rate makes U.S. debt and the U.S. dollar more attractive, raising U.S. external liabilities as a result. Conversely, a decline in the U.S. short-term interest rate makes U.S. debt and the U.S. dollar less attractive, lowering the U.S. external liabilities. Our sample from 2002 to 2019 is dominated by rate increases, first in the early 2000s and then again after the financial crisis, and the U.S. short-term interest rate at the end of 2019 is slightly higher than the interest rate in 2002. As a result, the overall effect is dominated by the rising policy rate that makes U.S. assets more attractive, leading to a more negative U.S. NFA position.
  Foreign monetary policy rates have been declining in our sample period. By a similar logic, this makes U.S. assets relatively more attractive for foreign investors and explains a further 4.1% per year widening in the U.S. NFA position.
  Up to this point, savings and issuances and monetary policies jointly explain counterfactual U.S. NFA dynamics with an average widening of 17.5% per year, which is roughly double the actual widening in the U.S. NFA of 9.4% per year. The last block in TableIIshows that changes in asset characteristics and latent demand partially offset the other primitive variables by shrinking the U.S. NFA by 8.2% per year.
  Within this block, we find that changes in characteristics had little effect on the aggregate trend in the U.S. NFA overall. Instead, changes in latent demand both within and across asset classes contributed to substantial narrowing (less negative) of the U.S. NFA. Changes in cross-asset-class latent demand were due to a shift in capital away from long-term debt investment toward equity investment globally. Since the U.S. is the preferred destination for bond investors, this shift toward the equity asset class narrowed the U.S. NFA position by an average of 2.9% per year.
  Shifts in within-asset-class latent demand further contributed to a narrowing of U.S. NFA by 4.6% per year. While, by definition, it is not immediately clear what features of the data within-asset-class latent demand capture, the influence of latent demand is useful for generating additional hypotheses about drivers of capital flows. For example, a recent paper by Atkeson, Heathcote, and Perri (2021) argues that a rise in the profitability of U.S. firms is important for explaining trends in international equity positions and returns. Unfortunately, we do not observe measures of firm profitability across our issuer country sample. Thus, the explanatory power of firm profitability falls largely into the latent demand block, which is an important factor offsetting the trends generated by savings and issuances, reserves, and policy rates. Our decomposition therefore provides a quantitative assessment of the potential effects of unobserved country characteristics after accounting for the other primitive explanatory factors.
  In the previous section, we highlight the main factors that explain the overall widening of the U.S. portfolio imbalance over the last two decades. Returning to Figure1, we observe that, on top of the overall downward trend in the U.S. NFA, the U.S. experienced a stark reversal in the valuation effects earned on its NFA position. Between 2002 and 2010, the U.S. earned a positive return on its NFA portfolio (i.e., an exorbitant privilege), which counterbalanced net capital inflows into the U.S. and contributed to a narrowing of the U.S. NFA position. However, from 2011 to 2019, the U.S. exorbitant privilege reversed, and the valuation effects further widened the U.S. NFA position.
  Building on our decomposition methodology, we first compute the valuation effects (Δj,tVal$\Delta ^{Val}_{j, t}$) on the U.S. NFA position by asset class before and after 2010 in TableIII. While much of the literature on global imbalances reports the return differences between U.S. external asset and external liability positions (e.g., Gourinchas and Rey (2007a) and Curcuru, Dvorak, and Warnock (2008)), we prefer our quantity-weighted decomposition methodology because it also takes into account the relative size of the U.S. asset and liability positions. Given that the U.S. liability position is notably larger than the U.S. asset position, a 1% return on the U.S. liability position has a much larger impact on the overall NFA position than a 1% return on the U.S. asset position.
  The top row in TableIIIreports the valuation effects on the U.S. NFA position. In the full sample, consistent with what we reported in TableII, this component helped reduce the impact of capital flows to the U.S. by 5.0% per year. The second column shows that between 2002 and 2010, valuation effects dampened the effects of capital inflows by an average of 18.3% per year. By contrast, the third column shows that valuation effects amplified the widening in the U.S. NFA position by an average of 6.9% per year post-2010.
  In the remainder of TableIII, we report the average returns on U.S. external assets and liabilities in different asset classes. For U.S. external assets, we also break down the returns into (foreign) local currency terms and exchange rate movements. Panel A shows that this reversal can be attributed to both a large decrease in the returns on U.S. external assets (from 10.5% to 1.7% per annum) and a slightly smaller increase in the returns on U.S. external liabilities (from 0.8% to 3.6%). About half of the decrease in the returns on U.S. external assets can be attributed to changes in returns in foreign currency units, and the other half to exchange rate movements.
  We now turn to a decomposition over time to understand the variables that explain the large reversal in valuation effects on the U.S. external portfolio after 2010. TableIVshows the contribution of each block of primitive variables in driving the valuation component of the U.S. external portfolio position pre- and post-2010. Three blocks of primitive variables show a large change between the two time periods: savings and issuances, reserves, and demand shifts and characteristics.
  The first two blocks of TableIVshow that changes in savings and issuances and central bank reserves between the first and second halves of our sample mostly offset each other. Prior to 2010, savings and issuances helped narrow the U.S. NFA position by 18.6% per year. After 2010, the contribution of savings and issuances to the narrowing of NFA dropped to 9.1% per year. This effect was driven primarily by declining U.S. issuance post-2010 relative to pre-2010, which led to relatively higher realized returns on U.S. liabilities post-2010. Changes to emerging market reserves display the opposite pattern. Emerging market central bank reserve accumulation prior to 2010 contributed to a 5% wider U.S. NFA per year. After 2010, this contribution declines to 0% per year as emerging market central bank reserve accumulation slowed.
  Another major contributor to the reversal in the valuation component was changes in latent demand, primarily within asset classes. The contribution of latent demand to the valuation component was 3.1% before 2010 and 12.5% after 2010, contributing 9.4% per year to the reversal. To understand the source of this change, Figure4presents the relative latent demand for U.S.-issued financial assets, relative to the latent demand for foreign-issued financial assets for both long-term debt and equity.21Increases in these measures imply stronger demand for U.S.-issued financial assets. As is evident from Figure4, the latent demand for U.S. long-term debt assets remained relatively flat over time, but latent demand for U.S. equity assets, relative to equity from different countries, exhibited a strong V-shaped pattern of much greater magnitude. Prior to 2010, U.S. equities became increasingly less desirable relative to foreign equities, which depressed U.S. equity values and the realized returns paid on equity liabilities. After 2010, however, this narrative completely reversed. A substantial increase in latent demand for U.S. equity captures a significant rise in U.S. equity returns, and a significant widening of the U.S. portfolio imbalance. Finally, we also note that changes in characteristics, specifically GDP and inflation, also contributed around 5% to the reversal.
  In sum, our decomposition of valuation effects shows that a number of forces coalesce to drive up realized returns of U.S. external liabilities and down returns on U.S. external assets. Together, these valuation forces contributed to a widening the U.S. external position over the last 10 years. Declines in U.S. asset issuances relative to the aggregate investor demand drive up the valuation of U.S. liabilities, while foreign private savings and central bank reserve accumulation lowers the valuation of U.S. liabilities. While these two effects offset each other, changes in asset characteristics and shifts toward U.S. equities further increase the return on U.S. liabilities relative to assets. Our decomposition not only clarifies the economic factors explaining the decline in the valuation effects earned on the U.S. NFA position, but also suggests that we should reevaluate the notion of exorbitant privilege entirely. Under the current definition, a decline in the U.S. exorbitant privilege is synonymous withincreasing demandfor the unobserved characteristics of U.S. financial assets.
  A potential concern is that our findings may be sensitive to the specific estimation and identification procedure we used for our demand equations (14) and (15). To demonstrate the robustness of our findings, we present two sets of results in theAppendix. First, we study the sensitivity of our decompositions to variations in our identification strategy. Second, we set the demand elasticities to a range of plausible values and show how our findings vary as we systematically iterate over this range. While the first set of results requires us to make alternative identification assumptions, the second set of results allows us to understand the robustness of our findings even in absence of a perfect identification strategy for our demand curves. Both exercises demonstrate that the conclusions drawn from our decompositions are robust to perturbations in estimated demand curves. The results where we vary the elasticities also permit us to further examine the mechanism through which our model operates, which we detail in theAppendix.
  Thus far, we have studied the sources of variation in the U.S. NFA position and its returns over the last 20 years. One salient feature of our estimated demand system is that it highlights the large differences in investor demand for financial assets issued by different countries. These differences give rise to substantial heterogeneity in a country's ability to borrow in international financial markets. In this section, we take a forward-looking perspective and ask: how much additional long-term debt can a country issue until its long-term yield increases by 1%. The results help identify a quantity dimension of the U.S. exorbitant privilege, which depends not only on the relatively high prices investors pay to hold U.S. assets, but also on the quantity of long-term debt that the U.S. can issue without affecting prices too much (Farhi and Maggiori (2017), He, Krishnamurthy, and Milbradt (2019), Jiang et al. (2019)).
  Formally, we take the state of the economy at the end of 2019 as given and conduct the following experiment. For each issuer country, we increase the amount of its long-term debt outstanding until its (endogenous) long-term yield increases by 1%. Figure5shows the results of this exercise for the G-10 countries. The left-hand panel shows that global investors have the greatest appetite for U.S. long-term debt in pure dollar amounts. Investors would absorb about 2.5 trillion dollars of U.S. long-term debt before requiring U.S. issuers to pay an additional 1% in yield. This amount is nearly triple the implied quantity for any other G-10 country, suggesting that the U.S. has the potential to extract more surplus from international bond markets.
  The right-hand panel of Figure5scales issuances by 2019 GDP and reveals that, although global investors are willing to absorb much more U.S. debt in absolute terms, the U.S. had much less room to issue debt at the end of 2019, after accounting for the size of the U.S. economy. Instead, our estimates suggest that Norway and the United Kingdom have the most room to issue long-term debt as a share of their respective GDP. In this sense, the glass is half full and half empty: The U.S. can still issue a fairly large amount of debt assets before yields go up, but this amount is quite small relative to the size of its economy.
article.V. Conclusion:
This paper uses a portfolio approach to evaluate the impact of savings and issuances, monetary policies, asset characteristics, and demand shifts on U.S. NFA dynamics. Our framework highlights three key insights that are important to consider for theories of global imbalances. First, the simple downward trend of the U.S. NFA position masks several countervailing forces. In particular, while global savings and central bank policies contribute to a widening in the U.S. NFA, shifts in asset characteristics and investor demand partially reverse this trend. Second, we observe a decline in the exorbitant privilege earned by the U.S. after 2010. We attribute this decline to an increase in global investor demand for U.S. equity, which drove up the realized returns of U.S. external liabilities. Finally, looking ahead, we show that demand for U.S. debt is quite stable. As a result, U.S. debt issuers have the privilege of issuing substantially more debt before suffering from higher yields. These results shed new light on the sources of the global imbalances, as well as reveal a new quantity dimension of U.S. privilege.
  Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
article.Appendix: Robustness to Variation in Dem:
SectionIII.Bpresents our baseline instrument construction and estimation. To ensure that our key findings are robust, we study how our results vary with different estimation procedures, instrument construction procedures, elasticities of demand, and cross-asset class substitution parameters. In this Appendix, we first present five alternative versions of our estimates with varying instrument constructions. Using these different estimates for the demand curves, we show that our key results are quantitatively similar across all specifications for demand curves. We further show that our quantitative findings are robust to using a range of demand elasticities that is consistent with those found in the literature, and we study the sensitivity of our decompositions to variation in the cross-asset-class demand parameters. Finally, we use these results to shine additional light on the model's mechanism.
  We relax the assumption that GDP is exogenous to latent demand. In particular, we allow country-level GDP to depend on asset prices, as would be the case in settings in which growth is related to capital flows.23We model country-level GDP as a function year fixed effects and prices (which are potentially a function of latent demand):
  
  We estimate this equation using our instruments for prices, where the instruments for prices are constructed as in our baseline procedure. Importantly, these instruments for prices do not use GDP to construct predicted weights. We then extract the residuals from this regression, which we refer to as GDP shocks. Finally, we estimate our within-asset-class equation, but instead of directly including log GDP as a characteristic we instrument for log GDP with these GDP shocks.
  The coefficients on expected returns and their standard errors for our baseline construction and these five variations can be found in TableAI. The point estimates vary across the specifications, though most are not notably different than each other. To understand how these differing estimates might impact our key results, we recompute our decompositions for each of these variations.
  TableAIIpresents the results of our trend decomposition as described in SectionIV.Cusing these five alternative specifications of instrument construction. The top panel presents the implied demand elasticities from these estimates.24The second panel presents our trend decomposition. The baseline column reiterates our decomposition of the changes in the U.S. NFA position that we discuss in SectionIV.C. Columns (1) through (5) present the alternative estimation and identification procedures along with the corresponding decompositions of the U.S. NFA position. As we look across the columns in TableAII, we see that the decomposition of trends in the U.S. NFA position does not vary substantially across the specifications. Total savings and issuances decrease the U.S. NFA position by an average of 7.3% to 11.8% per year, and reserves and policy rates decrease the U.S. NFA position by an additional 6.9% to 12.2% per year. Thus, the combination of these first three blocks always overshoots the widening observed in the U.S. NFA position in the data. Similarly, regardless of the specification, changes in asset characteristics and shifts in demand counteract the variables in the first three blocks by around 8% to 10%.
  We also study how variation in our estimation procedure impacts our decomposition of changes in valuation effects from SectionIV.D. Specifically, for the five alternative estimates, TableAIIIpresents the difference (post-2010 minus pre-2010) between the valuation contribution to U.S. NFA. We report the difference here because we are primarily interested in which components changed after 2010 that contributed to the decline in exorbitant privilege that we observe. Importantly, this table shows that the conclusions regarding the valuation effects are highly robust to specific estimated variations in demand curves. The main contributing variables to the decline in valuation effects on the U.S. NFA position continue to hold.
  In our second set of exercises, we show that even as we systematically vary the coefficient on the expected return in the demand curve, the decompositions of the trends in the U.S. NFA and the changes in returns both remain largely unchanged. These exercises also shine light on the model's mechanism, which we discuss here in detail. We focus on a range of coefficients on expected returns that imply demand elasticities that are consistent with those found in the recent literature that measures demand elasticities in financial markets. Gabaix and Koijen (2021) provide a discussion and summary of this literature.
  While there are few direct measures of the elasticities that we use in our paper, a reasonable benchmark for the elasticity of demand for country-level portfolios of long-term debt and equity is approximately 0.75 to 5.25We base this range on a number of facts. First, this range includes the range of demand elasticities shown in TableAII, which are direct estimates of our desired elasticities. Second, this range of estimates is consistent with those found in Koijen and Yogo (2020). Third, the literature on equity inclusion effects (e.g., Wurgler and Zhuravskaya (2002)) finds values in the range of one, although these estimates are based on individual stocks and on equity index inclusion, which limits their direct translation to our elasticities. Fourth, a more recent literature finds some elasticities to be below one (Gabaix and Koijen (2021)), which we include, although they are lower than we find across our various estimation procedures.
  To understand how our results vary across this range of demand elasticities, we take the minimum and maximum coefficients on expected returns from our various estimates and proportionally scale them up and down to achieve demand elasticities in this range for long-term debt and equity. To ensure that demand is consistent with the chosen coefficients on expected returns, we fix these coefficients and reestimate the remaining parameters in the demand curves. The first three rows of TableAIVpresent the elasticities that are implied by the coefficients on expected returns that we use for a range of seven different demand elasticities, along with our baseline values. Consistent with our targeted range, these elasticities range from 0.7 to 5.2 for long-term debt and 0.9 to 2.7 for equity.
  Each column of TableAIVpresents the decomposition of the U.S. NFA position, analogous to TableII, for different sets of coefficients on expected returns. To further understand the mechanism through which our model operates, we separately present the flow, valuation, and total (NFA) contribution of each step. We discuss the variation in the flow and valuation rows below. To assess the robustness of our findings, it is instructive to first study the NFA contribution of each of the four main blocks (Total Savings and Issuances, Total Reserves, Total Rates, Total Demand and Characteristics), which are bolded in the table. As we look across the columns of TableAIVfor each of these four rows, we see that the contribution of each set of primitive variables does not vary substantively across the various specifications of demand curves. Thus, our main qualitative results continue to hold under each of these alternative demand elasticities.
  TableAIVshines further light on the mechanism through which our model produces the quantitative values from our decomposition. As we vary the demand elasticities, this table illustrates how the separate contribution of the flow and valuation components of various primitive variables changes. In the following paragraphs, we explain how the flow, valuation, and NFA (the combined total) components change as we vary the elasticities. Recall that the flow and valuation components are additive in levels by year, whereas we are reporting the time-series average of the log values, which ensures that the NFA values sum across blocks. Thus, the sum of the flow and valuation components do not necessarily equal the NFA values.
  We first turn to U.S. savings and issuances. As discussed in SectionIV.C, the contribution of U.S. savings and issuances is driven primarily by the issuances component. The U.S. issued a large amount of assets relative to savings over this period that attracted foreign capital. Looking across the flow row for U.S. savings and issuances, we see that the contribution of the flow component is not particularly impacted by variation in the elasticity. Turning to the valuation component, we see that as demand becomes more elastic, the valuation component becomes less negative. This is because when demand is more elastic, the demand curve is flatter and the prices of U.S. foreign liabilities are less sensitive to the additional issuance that pushes down the value of U.S. foreign liabilities, making U.S. NFA less negative. The contribution to U.S. NFA is the combined contribution of the flow and valuation components, which is increasing in the demand elasticity. This increasing relation of the contribution of U.S. savings and issuances is driven primarily by the impact of higher demand elasticities dampening the effect of issuances on U.S. liabilities.
  The impact of foreign savings and issuances on the U.S. NFA are largely due to foreign savings in excess of local investment opportunities (issuances) being directed toward the U.S. When capital flows toward the U.S., it increases U.S. liabilities through the flow component. Looking across the foreign savings and issuances flow row we see that the flow effect is fairly stable as we vary the elasticities, similar to the U.S. savings and issuances flow effect. As we increase the demand elasticities, we see that the valuation effect decreases. With the most elastic demand in column (7), the valuation component tends to shrink the U.S. NFA, whereas with the least elastic demand in column (1), the valuation component tends to widen the U.S. NFA position. Separate inspection of assets and liabilities reveals that the valuation component for U.S. external assets is quite stable as we vary elasticities, as U.S. investors substitute across the many countries' assets that make up the U.S. foreign asset portfolio. On the other hand, the magnitude of the valuation component of U.S. external liabilities is decreasing in the demand elasticities. Taken together, this generates the decreasing pattern we observe between the valuation component of U.S. NFA and the demand elasticities.
  Looking at the total effects of savings and issuances as we vary the elasticities, we see that the flow effect does not vary substantially as we increase or decrease the elasticities. We also see that the valuation effect does not vary substantially with elasticities. This is driven by the fact that the foreign savings and issuances impact of the valuation effect is decreasing in elasticities, whereas the U.S. savings and issuances impact of the valuation effect is increasing in elasticities. These two effects tend to offset each other such that the overall effect of global savings and issuances on U.S. is relatively stable as we vary demand elasticities, including for the separate flow and savings components.
  Turning to reserves, the flow component of U.S. QE tends to narrow the U.S. NFA position, which is due to the fact that QE lowers the effective supply of U.S. liabilities. With lower effective supply, U.S. external liabilities become more expensive (widening valuation effect) and foreign countries tend to shift their portfolios away from the U.S. (narrowing flow effect). These effects are stronger when demand is more inelastic. This also explains why the valuation impact of U.S. QE, which tends to widen the NFA position, is decreasing in the demand elasticities.
  Foreign reserves tend to hold U.S. long-term debt and these holdings were increasing over our sample period. As a result, the flow component of foreign reserves tends to widen U.S. NFA. These purchases of U.S. external liabilities increase their price. The relation between the valuation component of foreign reserves and U.S. NFA is relatively stable, as foreign countries tend to substitute away from the U.S. when U.S. financial liabilities became more expensive.
  Higher interest rates in the U.S. relative to the rest of the world tended to draw capital toward the U.S. When demand curves are more elastic, foreign investors respond more to the interest rate differential, which pulls more capital into the U.S. and increases the magnitude of the flow effect. In general the valuation effect is negative for the policy rate moves, because U.S. investors increase their external asset position whenever foreigners invest more in U.S. external liabilities, which increases the value of the U.S. external asset portfolio. The increase in the prices on foreign liabilities in response to U.S. flows tends to be the dominating force. The magnitude of the valuation effect increases with the magnitude of the demand elasticity, as investors become more responsive to the flows. However, the effect of interest rates on the NFA is still positive, and is driven by the flows of capital entering the U.S.
  Changes in latent demand narrowed the U.S. NFA position because demand shifted toward more foreign liabilities on average over the sample period. The valuation effect partially counters the flows of capital out of the U.S., because U.S. investors buy up U.S. external liabilities whenever foreigners sell. The magnitude of the flow effect increases with the demand elasticity because the effect of price differentials is amplified. This amplification in the magnitude of flows also amplifies the magnitude of the corresponding valuation effects. All told, the flow effects dominate and the NFA position narrows in response to changes in latent demand.
  TableAVpresents the return decomposition exercise for various scalings of the coefficient on expected returns. For this table, we also separately present the valuation contribution from 2002 to 2010, 2011 to 2019, and their difference. Again, as we look across the rows of the table that report the difference, the variables most responsible for the decrease in the valuation effect over time are consistent across all specifications. Thus, we conclude that our decomposition of the valuation effects on the U.S. NFA position is also robust to a variety of parameterizations of the demand curve.
  As a final set of robustness checks, we fix the estimated within-asset-class demand curves to our baseline values and vary the cross-asset substitution parameters,λ$\lambda$.26TableAVIpresents the results for the trend decomposition. With the exception of interest rates, the various components are not particularly sensitive to the cross-asset-class substitution parameters. For interest rates, we see that as we decrease the amount of segmentation (increasing theλ$\lambda$parameters), the flow component tends to increase. This is because investors are more likely to substitute away from short-term debt and toward the other two asset classes, increasing the impact of higher U.S. rates relative to the rest of the world on the U.S. NFA.
  Finally, TableAVIIpresents the difference between the valuation component pre- and post-2010. Overall, these tables show that our results are robust to variation in the estimated cross-asset demand curves as well.
